The first computer bug was an actual bug — a moth found trapped in a relay of the Harvard Mark II in 1947.
The average person unlocks their phone 96 times a day — once every 10 minutes.
There are over 1.13 billion websites on the internet, but fewer than 200 million are actively maintained.
The first 1GB hard drive, released in 1980, weighed 550 lbs and cost $40,000.
Email is older than the World Wide Web. Ray Tomlinson sent the first networked email in 1971.
The @ symbol was chosen for email addresses because it was the least-used character on the keyboard at the time.
It would take about 1.1 trillion years to crack a 128-bit AES encryption key by brute force.
The first computer virus, 'Creeper', appeared in 1971 and simply displayed: 'I'm the creeper, catch me if you can!'
Wi-Fi doesn't actually stand for 'Wireless Fidelity' — the name was coined by a branding agency and was never an acronym.
The world's first domain name ever registered was symbolics.com on March 15, 1985.
Over 3.5 billion Google searches are made every single day — roughly 40,000 per second.
The original iPhone had no App Store — Apple's Steve Jobs initially insisted web apps were enough.
CAPTCHA stands for Completely Automated Public Turing test to tell Computers and Humans Apart.
The first webcam was used at Cambridge University to monitor a coffee pot — so researchers didn't walk downstairs for an empty pot.
Linux powers over 96% of the world's top 1 million servers.
The term 'bug' in software predates computers — it was used by Thomas Edison in 1878 to describe flaws in his inventions.
GPS satellites are so precise that engineers must account for Einstein's theory of relativity to keep them accurate.
Python was named after Monty Python's Flying Circus, not the snake.
A strand of fiber optic cable thinner than a human hair can carry more than 10 terabits of data per second.
The first hard drive IBM shipped with a PC in 1983 held just 10 megabytes — enough for about two MP3 songs.
The average computer user blinks only 7 times per minute while staring at a screen — the normal rate is 20.
Nintendo was founded in 1889 — as a playing card company.
The world's first computer programmer was Ada Lovelace, who wrote an algorithm for Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine in 1843.
Hewlett-Packard was started in a garage in Palo Alto in 1939 with just $538 in startup capital.
The first text message ever sent read 'Merry Christmas' and was sent on December 3, 1992.
About 90% of the world's currency exists only digitally — as ones and zeros in bank computers.
The word 'robot' comes from the Czech word 'robota', meaning forced labor or drudgery.
The first computer mouse was made of wood, invented by Doug Engelbart in 1964.
YouTube was originally conceived as a video dating site — it pivoted after almost no one uploaded dating videos.
A standard Rubik's Cube has 43 quintillion possible combinations but can always be solved in 20 moves or fewer.
The internet weighs about 50 grams — that's the estimated mass of all the electrons in motion at any moment.
Spam email gets its name from a Monty Python sketch where the word 'spam' is repeated incessantly.
The Apollo 11 guidance computer had less processing power than a modern USB charger.
More people in the world have access to a mobile phone than to a toilet.
Amazon originally called itself 'Cadabra' — until the founder's lawyer misheard it as 'Cadaver'.
Every minute, users send over 240 million emails, post 500,000 tweets, and upload 500 hours of YouTube video.
The QR code was invented in 1994 by a Toyota subsidiary to track car parts on the assembly line.
The first computer to beat a reigning chess world champion was IBM's Deep Blue, which defeated Garry Kasparov in 1997.
Roughly 15% of all Google searches have never been searched before — every day.
The USB standard took 7 years to develop — largely because of arguments about which side of the connector should be 'up'.
